retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Macrosomia refers to excessive fetal growth, typically defined by an absolute birth weight rather than gestational age. Historically, birth weights of 4,000 g or 4,500 g have been used to classify macrosomia, though no universal definition has been accepted. Maternal and neonatal risks increase significantly with higher birth weights. Prenatal prediction of macrosomia is challenging. Ultrasonography, the most common tool for estimating fetal weight, has limited accuracy, with errors increasing as fetal weight rises. Clinical assessment methods are similarly unreliable, as factors like maternal obesity can lead to overestimations or inconsistent results.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Evaluation -- Maternal Gestational Diabetes Screening and Diagnostic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with a positive 50-g glucose screen, a diagnostic test using a 100-g 3-hour OGTT is necessary. [11] The following values are used as parameters for abnormal results for a 3-hour OGTT: Fasting: ≥95 mg/dL First hour: ≥180 mg/dL Second hour: ≥155 mg/dL Third hour: ≥140 mg/dL [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A patient who delivers a macrosomic infant should be screened very carefully for previously undiagnosed diabetes. If such screening is negative, they should be monitored carefully in subsequent pregnancies. The goal of scheduled cesarean birth for suspected macrosomia is to reduce fetal morbidity or maternal morbidity, or both. Although fetal and maternal morbidity increases with birth weights >4,000 g, most births of macrosomic newborns are uncomplicated. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Macrosomia refers to excessive fetal growth, typically defined by an absolute birth weight rather than gestational age, and is an obstetric condition associated with other potentially life-threatening complications to both the mother and the neonate. [1] The term macrosomia is derived from the Greek words macro , meaning big, and somia (body). The earliest use of the term was from the work of Robley Dunglison (1798-1859), an English physician and medical writer. According to the American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology (ACOG), this condition contrasts with "large for gestational age" (LGA), which considers birth weight relative to gestational age, specifically at or above the 90th percentile. Historically, birth weights of 4,000 g or 4,500 g have been used to classify macrosomia, though no universal definition has been accepted. [2] [3] [4] Maternal and neonatal risks increase significantly with higher birth weights. For infants weighing between 4,000 and 4,499 g, a moderate rise in complications, eg, birth injuries and labor abnormalities, have been demonstrated. However, morbidity and mortality risks, including stillbirth and long-term complications like brachial plexus injury, escalate sharply for weights exceeding 4,500 g and are highest above 5,000 g.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Evaluation -- Maternal Gestational Diabetes Screening and Diagnostic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, in patients who are identified as having high-risk pregnancies or clinical suspicion of a pregnancy complication (eg, fundal height greater than gestational age), ultrasound imaging may be performed for diagnostic evaluation or as part of antenatal surveillance. Typically, fetal growth assessments require serial ultrasounds performed at at least 4-week intervals. [14] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation ," for further information. Furthermore, antenatal fetal surveillance may be indicated in patients with medication-controlled or uncontrolled diabetes. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal prediction of macrosomia is challenging. Ultrasonography, the most common tool for estimating fetal weight, has limited accuracy, with errors increasing as fetal weight rises. For example, ultrasound's ability to predict birth weights over 4,500 g is imprecise, with sensitivity ranging from 33% to 44%. Clinical methods, eg, abdominal palpation, are similarly unreliable. Factors like maternal obesity further complicate these predictions, as they can lead to overestimations or inconsistent results. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify fetal macrosomia risk factors. Interpret diagnostic findings associated with fetal macrosomia. Implement management approaches in patients with suspected fetal macrosomia. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients with fetal macrosomia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses that should also be considered when evaluating macrosomia include: Polyhydramnios Inaccurate estimation of gestational age Multiple gestations Uterine anatomic lesions, eg, uterine myoma, adenomyosis Pelvic masses (eg, ovarian masses) Morbid obesity Postterm pregnancies

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations that may be indicated for macrosomia management include: Obstetric specialist Maternal-fetal medicine specialists Endocrinologists Nutritional therapists [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, recognizing early indicators of this condition is essential because GDM is a significant risk factor for macrosomia. Because early screening is indicated by clinical history and assessment, documenting past medical history, obstetric outcomes, and family history of type 2 diabetes are essential components of GDM assessment. The clinical features of GDM can be varied. The disproportionate weight gain, obesity, and elevated body mass index (BMI) can be suggestive features. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Evaluation -- Neonatal Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical evaluation of the neonate's respiratory effort after birth is also essential as meconium aspiration due to fetal distress and transient tachypnea of the newborn (TTN) are common and tend to occur 2 to 3 times more frequently in macrocosmic babies, especially if secondary to GDM.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal factors Diabetes : Diabetes in pregnancy includes gestational diabetes (GDM), insulin-dependent, or drug-induced diabetes. Jordan Pederson, in 1920, hypothesized that maternal hyperglycemia is associated with fetal hyperinsulinemia and fetal hyperglycemia, which ultimately leads to the overutilization of glucose by the fetus and, hence, the abnormal increase in growth. [5] Obesity : Globally, there is a current epidemic of obesity. Obesity constitutes a significant risk for diabetes in all demographics. Precisely, maternal obesity is linked to a 4- to 12-fold increase in the prospect of fetal macrosomia. The standard metabolic basis of macrosomia is believed to be increased insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia. Abnormalities in maternal lipid levels may also play an essential role in the development of macrosomia. [6] Multiparity : Compared to other maternal risk factors, multiparity is not a major risk factor for macrosomia. Still, it can contribute to maternal diabetes and obesity, which are more important causes. Women with parity greater than 3 are prone to have macrosomic babies. [7] An associated 100 to 150-g weight gain can be observed with each pregnancy, thus increasing the risk of macrosomia in the long term in this group of patients. Previous LGA infants : women with previous macrocosmic babies are at a 5- to 10-fold increased risk of another macrosomic baby. Postterm pregnancy : prolonged gestation of more than 42 weeks is more likely linked with an increased chance of macrosomia due to the continuous supply of nutrients and oxygen-rich blood to the developing fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed physical examination should include monitoring of patient weight at each prenatal visit that should be correlated with suggested United States Institute of Medicine guidelines (IOM) as follows: A weight gain of 28 to 40 lbs (12-18 kg) for patients with a BMI of <18  kg/m 2 A weight gain of 25 to 35 lbs (11.5-16 kg) for patients with a BMI between 18.5 to 24.9 kg/m 2 A weight gain of 15 to 25 lbs (7-11.5 kg) for patients with a BMI between 25.0 to 29.9 kg/m 2 A weight gain of 11 to 20 lbs (5-9 kg) for patients with a BMI >30 kg/m 2

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy is a physiologic state that requires close monitoring and evaluation from the time of initial diagnosis to the expulsion of all products of conception. High-risk pregnancies require close monitoring, with varied management when indicated. Therefore, antenatal care involves continued assessment for the timely identification of risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrosomia -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: The ACOG recommends against delivery before 39 0/7 weeks of gestation unless medically indicated. At this time, and until additional studies are reported, suspected macrosomia or LGA fetus is not an indication for induction of labor before 39 0/7 weeks of gestation because of insufficient evidence that the benefits of reducing shoulder dystocia risk would outweigh the harms of early delivery. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, a condition denoting fetal growth restriction. This classification highlights the interrelated nature of these perinatal indicators, which together signal impaired intrauterine growth and may necessitate focused obstetric monitoring and intervention to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively classified as an extremely premature birth of unspecified weight.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2435
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine size for dates discrepancy is a specific type of finding related to pregnancy, indicating that the measured uterine size does not correspond with the expected gestational dates. This obstetric finding alerts clinicians to possible issues such as inaccurate dating of the pregnancy, abnormal fetal growth (including growth restriction or macrosomia), or the presence of multiple gestations, and therefore plays a key role in guiding further diagnostic evaluation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1610
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with الولادة القيصرية (Cesarean delivery) and with قصر القامة (short stature). In addition, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة. These relationships indicate that short stature is linked both to infants born preterm with low birth weight and to the condition of threatened preterm labor, while preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are more likely to be delivered by Cesarean section. Recognizing these interconnections can help clinicians anticipate delivery method needs and monitor growth outcomes in infants at risk for or experiencing preterm birth complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with a threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر). Recognizing these connections can help clinicians monitor growth patterns and anticipate obstetric complications, thereby improving patient care.
